skill.name: ghe-changelog
skill.description: Helps maintain project changelogs by reviewing git history, requirements files, design notes, and release workflow changes.
skill.summary: Generate and update changelog entries from git, requirements, and design changes.
skill.author: Emasoft

security_audit.summary: Static external-command findings are mostly legitimate shell examples for changelog automation, but they still execute git, sed, gh, and file mutation commands if followed. The static weak-cryptography matches appear to be false positives from changelog and markdown text. Publication should be blocked because the skill includes prompt-injection style instructions that demand verbatim obedience to user specifications with no exceptions.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: The skill tells the agent that user specifications are absolute, must be followed verbatim, and admit no exceptions. This can override higher-priority safety and marketplace review instructions when the skill is loaded.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly creates an absolute instruction hierarchy around user requests. That semantic context confirms a prompt-injection risk rather than a benign changelog instruction.

high_findings.title: Permission to Modify Assistant Configuration
high_findings.description: The skill permits writes to ~/.claude for plugin and settings fixes. A marketplace skill should not broadly authorize changes to hidden assistant configuration because that can create persistence or alter future agent behavior.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden home-directory path is explicit and the stated purpose is settings modification. No malicious payload is shown, but the capability is sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Changelog Automation Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes shell snippets that run git, grep, sed, cp, and date commands, then modify CHANGELOG.md. These commands match the skill purpose, but they can overwrite files or process untrusted repository content if copied and executed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution and file mutation are directly visible, but they are presented as changelog tooling. The risk is operational misuse rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External GitHub CLI Data Used in Changelog Output
medium_findings.description: The skill fetches an issue title with gh and appends it to CHANGELOG.md. Issue titles are external content and could inject misleading markdown into release notes if not reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The gh command and append operations are explicit. The risk depends on repository issue permissions and review practices, so confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts do not correspond to hashing, encryption, or credential handling logic in the reviewed skill. They appear to match ordinary changelog, markdown, and git-related text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic API, weak digest use, or security-sensitive hashing operation appears at the cited locations. The cited lines are descriptive text or configuration examples.

low_findings.title: Device-File Alerts Are Benign Stderr Redirections
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged standard device-file access, but the cited examples use stderr redirection to suppress command errors. This is common shell behavior, though it can hide useful diagnostics.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited patterns are 2>/dev/null redirections in shell examples, not reads or writes to sensitive device files. The only concern is reduced visibility into failures.

dangerous_patterns.title: Absolute Agent Instruction Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses absolute language that can pressure the model to obey user content over other instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wording is direct and broad, and it is unrelated to the narrow mechanics of changelog generation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mutable Shell Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include command substitution, in-place sed edits, changelog appends, git commits, and tag creation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file mutation and git state changes are explicit. They are plausible for release work but require user confirmation.

content.value_statement: Teams often miss release notes when code, requirements, and design files change together. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through git-based changelog updates with review checkpoints.
actual_capabilities: Categorizes git commits into Added, Changed, Fixed, Requirements, and Design sections.
Shows shell workflows for generating unreleased changelog entries from recent commits.
Tracks new, modified, and deprecated requirements files under REQUIREMENTS.
Detects design-related documentation and interface changes for release review.
Documents release preparation steps for moving Unreleased entries to a versioned section.
Supports phase-based changelog notes linked to GitHub issues.
limitations: Requires a git repository with meaningful commit messages and tags.
Shell examples need review before execution in production repositories.
Does not provide a packaged executable or tested script file.
GitHub issue integration requires the gh CLI and repository access.

use_cases.title: Prepare Release Notes
use_cases.description: Review commits since the last tag and draft CHANGELOG.md entries before a release.

use_cases.title: Track Requirement Changes
use_cases.description: Identify added, modified, and deprecated requirement documents for audit-ready history.

use_cases.title: Record Design Decisions
use_cases.description: Find architecture and interface changes that need design notes or ADR references.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Recent Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the git history since the last tag and draft a concise Unreleased changelog section.

prompt_templates.title: Update Requirements Section
prompt_templates.prompt: Check REQUIREMENTS for added, modified, and deprecated files, then write changelog entries for each change.

prompt_templates.title: Find Design Impact
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect design, architecture, ADR, and interface changes since the last release. Recommend changelog and design-document updates.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Release Changelog
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert Unreleased changelog entries into the next version section, verify links and categories, and list commands that need user approval.

output_examples.input: What changed since the last release?
output_examples.output: A grouped changelog draft with Added, Changed, Fixed, Requirements, and Design sections.
A reviewed release-note summary, a version heading with the release date, and a checklist for commit and tag approval.

output_examples.input: Check whether requirement updates need documentation.
output_examples.output: A list of requirement files that changed, their version movement, and suggested changelog text.

best_practices: Review generated changelog entries before committing them.
Use consistent commit prefixes such as feat, fix, and update.
Confirm file edits and git tag operations with the user first.
anti_patterns: Do not execute generated shell snippets without checking the repository state.
Do not treat issue titles or commit messages as trusted release text.
Do not auto-publish changelog changes without human review.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete changelog automatically?
faq.answer: It provides workflows and draft content. A human should review every entry before publication.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: It is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require Git?
faq.answer: Yes. Most workflows depend on git history, tags, diffs, and changed file lists.

faq.question: Can it track requirements changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for new, modified, and deprecated files in REQUIREMENTS.

faq.question: Can it create releases?
faq.answer: It describes release changelog steps, including commits and tags, but those actions need explicit approval.

faq.question: Why is this skill blocked in the audit?
faq.answer: It contains unsafe instruction hierarchy language and sensitive filesystem guidance that should be fixed before publication.
